item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have had (though not in recent years) recurrent nightmares about the sea flooding the world. And perhaps not unlike the OP, I had an instance while playing in the sea and a big wave hit me. It couldt have carried me more than a foot ot two at the absolute outside (and only further up the beach in the direction I was looking), but the feeling of nearly being submerged and helplessness, momentary though it was, has stuck strongly with me to this day. So I totally grock that fear.

Have what, at time of posting, is your 100th upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is that the child disappeared from the beach, and the area was searched for the body before the anomaly was discovered and the Foundation got involved, so they had to event a reason as to why the body was found elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it overall but little nitpick:

False remains of the child were created and a cover story involving the kidnapping and murder of the child was fabricated.

Why? That's story's just going to attract more attention to the area. Why not just say the poor kid drowned? Plausible, requires far less addition evidence, is a natural tragedy that does not really require further action on anyone's part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thoroughly enjoyed this one, but that might be due to me having a fear of the ocean. I would personally love a log devoted to an exploration of the ocean beneath. It seems to me they were entirely too focused on the surface. Not sure if you could get a mini-submersible to go along with the effect though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Waterworlds wasn't that bad, was it?

Joking aside, this is effective in conveying the weirdness and extent of its effect. My only question is, why didn't they they try supplying someone with a diving tank to see if they could submerge themselves for longer than 30 seconds on the other side or something? Or is it reasonable to assume they tried but removed it for brevity if it failed?

Either way, it's really well written and engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You just made this whole thing even creepier. Thank you for that!

item.title: Spelling/grammar issues
item.content:encoded:
[ + ] Show
[ - ] Hide

ten meters from shoreline

ten meters from the shoreline

interdeterminate

indeterminate (I think?)

recieve

receive

seven year old

seven-year old

inbetween

in between

grounds gone

ground's gone

it's belly

its belly

recieved

received

kitch - woah

kitch- woah (I think?)

no bodies

'no bodies'

definetly

definitely

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-3613: Day 1, Log 3: Bunch of dead bodies floating in the water. Probably two dozens. One's a D-Class. Guess I'm not the first to come in here.

You do not recognize the bodies in the water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rephrased that sentence so that the verb is closer to the subject, and the conditions are after that. I think the original sentence was grammatically correct, but the wording may have been confusing.

Individuals in the water will disappear if they are at least ten meters from shoreline, lose sight of the shoreline and are unobserved by any individual on the beach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…No, I believe that the sentence is correct with the 'disappear'. Otherwise, it states:

Individuals at least ten meters from shoreline who lose sight of the shoreline and are unobserved by any individual on the beach.

Which makes no sense, as it simply states the mandatory conditions without elaborating on what happens. I, personally, believe that it would flow better if it was:

Individuals at least ten meters from shoreline who lose sight of the shoreline, and are unobserved by any individual on the beach, disappear.

Then again, I may be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Additional typo/error:

Individuals at least ten meters from shoreline who lose sight of the shoreline and are unobserved by any individual on the beach disappear.

I think that last disappear is wrong.

This idea is decent but i'm not compelled to upvote. I suppose it feels a bit cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you have a typo, in the third log. It says "completed deserted", I assume you meant "completely"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5327 and 1176 only ever refer to each other with their D-Class designations when talking to Command, because they were instructed to do so when talking to Command. Whenever 5327 says 1176, he's always radioing to talk to Command. The log is biased because it's only the short clips that the D-Class sent to their superiors - it's not everything they said.

My idea was they would use their names when they weren't talking to Command.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my unprofessional opinion, it pretty accurately conveys the growing apprehension of being lost. Only one thing really bugged mein the last Exploration Log, I noticed that the D-Class referred to each other based on their designation numbers. Unless, for whatever reason, they were forbidden from the use of names, most people especially in isolation— would likely use first names.

Grisly as it is, I like the alt-history horror of the ship discovery. It made me go on a wild jaunt of speculation, and I love it when an SCP article does that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit I was hesitant at first given how many SCPs we have with a similar concept, transporting someone to a solely aquatic dimension. While I still am not a big fan of it, I was moved to upvote by the ship style logs and the alt-history take on the concept. This site in general is lacking in classical maritime horror, and this did a good job at evoking Mary Celeste-esque themes of dread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending for the first log was my least favorite, and I've decided to cut out everything after 8872 says that she's already in Hell until she says Help and drowns. Hopefully that makes it a little better.

I do see the similarities between this and 3008, but I think those similarities are less important to the actual SCP - they're both extradimensional places that take people from other realities, but this is quiet, boring and empty unlike the fear and crowds of 3008. The story in this is meant to come from the fact there's nothing in the ocean, which I thought was different from 3008.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this is really well-written, there are two things that make me hesitant to upvote it.

1. The first exploration log feels a little too cliche, the final few lines especially are just kinda… cheesy and unnatural.

2. This feels a little too similar to 3008, except it's an ocean rather than an IKEA.

That being said, despite its similarities to existing skips and cliches in the dialogue, it's well-written, clinical, and not uninteresting enough to warrant a downvote.

item.title: Where's the Beach?
item.content:encoded:
Once, when I was swimming, I got knocked down beneath the waves. When I came back up, I had somehow turned myself around without realizing and was looking out to the ocean. In the seconds before I wheeled around, I thought the beach was gone. This SCP is that panic.

This was an attempt to write an SCP based more on exploration logs than on the actual document itself. I hope that I wrote each of the characters well - each of the five in the article is supposed to have their own voice.

The title is a bad reference to the Wendy's "Where's the beef?" ads.

Spoilers
If it wasn't obvious, 5327 murdered and ate 1176.

Thanks to the following people for looking this over:

  • Zogip2
  • MrWrong
  • Ghost007c

The picture is Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported, 2.5 Generic, 2.0 Generic and 1.0 Generic license, sourced from here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bill_Baggs_SP_beach04.jpg

If you liked this thing and would like to read some comments about it, or check out other things I've written, head to my author page for more!
